query: how to compare two objects in javascript

competitors.label: MDN Strict equality
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for object identity via `===`, which is the first branch of object comparison decisions.
competitors.winReason: Our page is broader on comparison strategy because it distinguishes identity, shallow, deep, and JSON pitfalls, while MDN only covers the identity branch.
theirStrengths: It states the core identity rule correctly: object equality with `===` is reference equality.
theirGaps: It is not a full object-comparison guide and does not move into shallow vs deep comparison strategies.

realWorldUseCases: Object comparison in JavaScript is not one single operation. You choose the cheapest comparison strategy that matches your intent:
followUpConfusion: Most interview mistakes happen when people use === but expect deep value equality, or when they deep-compare everything even though a flat shallow check would have been enough.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the identity rule correctly, but it does not treat comparison as a choose-the-right-strategy problem.
actionableExamples: It gives equality examples, but not a practical strategy comparison, so the example criterion remains close rather than clearly behind.
followUpConfusion: It does not answer the next question of when shallow or deep comparison is the right tool.

actionableExamples: Add one compact strategy table row for library-based deep equality if we want a clearer example edge over the identity-only docs.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Deep Equal
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant deep-equality interview problem, so it should calibrate our answer against a strong interview-prep competitor.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is strong on implementing deep equal, but our page now wins the broader interview question because it explains when identity, shallow, and deep comparison are each the right choice and adds explicit business-case examples.
theirStrengths: It turns deep equality into a concrete implementation challenge with clear examples and testable behavior.
theirGaps: It is narrower on the earlier decision of when shallow or identity comparison is enough.

realWorldUseCases: Object comparison in JavaScript is not one single operation. You choose the cheapest comparison strategy that matches your intent:
GreatFrontEnd is very relevant for deep equality, but it is narrower on the earlier decision of whether deep comparison is needed at all.
actionableExamples: Its implementation challenge is strong, while our page now adds both a shallow-equality business case and a nested deep-comparison example.
followUpConfusion: GreatFrontEnd still handles the deep-equality implementation follow-up well enough that the broader follow-up set remains close.
Add one note on Map/Set comparison policy if we want a broader advanced follow-up edge than the current dates/functions/cycles note.

competitors.label: GeeksforGeeks Compare Objects
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a common mainstream competitor that surveys multiple comparison techniques in one page.
competitors.winReason: GeeksforGeeks surveys many methods, but our page now wins this comparison because it turns the topic into a strategy choice, then backs that up with shallow, deep, and JSON-order examples tied to real UI trade-offs.
theirStrengths: It surveys several comparison approaches, including reference checks, `JSON.stringify`, `Object.keys`, and recursive comparison.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about choosing the comparison strategy based on intent.

realWorldUseCases: If a filter bar stores only flat primitive fields like page, sort, and query, a shallow comparison is cheaper and easier to reason about than a recursive deep compare.
GeeksforGeeks covers many methods, but our page is stronger about picking the cheapest method that matches the business intent.
actionableExamples: Its method inventory is broad, while our page now adds a concrete shallow-equality business case plus a JSON key-order trap.
followUpConfusion: The tutorial covers many approaches, so the broader follow-up set remains close instead of clearly behind.
Add one short note on comparing class instances with semantic equality rules if we want a broader advanced follow-up set.
